name: Peter Lynch GARP (Growth at a Reasonable Price)
source: One Up On Wall Street (Peter Lynch, 1989) + extracted rules from Bill's NotebookLM analysis
extracted_by: Claude Opus via NotebookLM Lynch framework
philosophy: Don't overpay for growth. A P/E ratio is only meaningful relative to the company's growth rate. The sweet spot is sustainable 15-25% growth at a PEG below 1.0, funded by a strong balance sheet.

rules.description: PEG ratio is in the buy zone

interpretation.pass: PEG between 0.5 and 1.0 — undervalued relative to growth
interpretation.fail_high: PEG above 1.0 — paying too much for growth
interpretation.fail_low: PEG below 0.5 — check for earnings cliff or one-time boost

rules.description: PEG ratio is not in the avoid zone

interpretation.pass: PEG below 2.0 — not in Lynch's avoid zone
interpretation.fail: PEG above 2.0 — overvalued in Lynch framework, high risk of multiple compression

rules.description: Earnings growth is in the 15-25% sustainable sweet spot

interpretation.pass: Growth in Lynch's sustainable sweet spot
interpretation.fail_low: Growth under 15% — stalwart territory, limited upside
interpretation.fail_high: Growth above 25% — high deceleration risk, attracts competition

rules.description: P/E ratio is below 20 (not priced for perfection)

interpretation.fail: P/E above 20 — may still qualify if PEG is favorable, but elevated risk

rules.description: Debt-to-equity ratio is below 0.30 (strong balance sheet)

interpretation.pass: Balance sheet meets Lynch's conservative threshold
interpretation.fail: Debt load may amplify downside in a downturn

notes: v1 is deliberately strict on PEG (two separate rules) because PEG is the mathematical anchor of the framework. The inventory/receivables check from Lynch's book is not included in v1 because it requires line-item balance sheet access that FMP's free tier doesn't expose. Add in v2 if the data source is upgraded.
